text:
COMBINED IMAGING REPORT
Technetium-99m Methylene Diphosphonate (MDP) Whole-Body Bone Scintigraphy
Computed Tomography of the Chest, Abdomen, and Pelvis with Intravenous Contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Raymond Kowalski, 71-year-old male (age 67 at time of study). Newly diagnosed adenocarcinoma of the prostate with Gleason score 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5. PSA 187 ng/mL. Clinical stage cT3b on MRI (bilateral seminal vesicle invasion). Bilateral pelvic lymphadenopathy on prior MRI. Bone scan and CT requested for complete metastatic staging prior to initiation of systemic therapy.

TECHNIQUE:
Bone Scan: 25.9 mCi of Tc-99m MDP administered intravenously. Whole-body planar images obtained in anterior and posterior projections at approximately 3 hours post-injection. Supplemental SPECT/CT images obtained through the pelvis, lumbar spine, and thoracic spine for anatomic correlation.

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: Helical CT performed following the administration of 120 mL of nonionic iodinated intravenous contrast (iopamidol). Oral contrast administered. Images obtained in the portal venous phase through the abdomen and pelvis and in routine phase through the chest. Multiplanar reformations generated. Note: dose-adjusted protocol employed given known chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL). Adequate pre-hydration administered per nephrology-approved protocol.

---

BONE SCAN FINDINGS:

Overall Pattern:
Abnormal whole-body bone scan demonstrating multiple discrete foci of markedly increased radiotracer uptake in the axial and appendicular skeleton, consistent with multifocal osteoblastic osseous metastatic disease. The distribution is heterogeneous and focal rather than diffusely confluent; no superscan pattern is identified. Normal physiologic radiotracer activity is seen in the kidneys, bladder, and soft tissues.

Specific Lesions:

Spine:
- T9 vertebral body: A focal area of markedly increased radiotracer accumulation is identified in the T9 vertebral body at the midline. SPECT/CT correlation confirms tracer uptake centrally within the vertebral body without involvement of the posterior elements or evidence of cortical disruption on the CT component.
- L2 vertebral body: Discrete focus of intensely increased uptake in the L2 vertebral body. SPECT/CT localization confirms central vertebral body involvement. No collapse or compromise of the vertebral height on CT component.

Pelvis / Iliac Bones:
- Left iliac wing: A moderately-to-markedly intense focus of increased uptake is identified in the posterior left iliac wing. SPECT/CT correlation demonstrates a corresponding area of sclerotic bony change.
- Right acetabulum: Focal increased uptake in the right acetabulum, most intense in the superolateral aspect. CT correlation shows subtle sclerotic thickening at this site. No fracture or cortical breach identified at this time.

Ribs:
- Right 4th rib: A focus of mildly-to-moderately increased uptake is identified along the posterior arc of the right 4th rib. The focality and intensity are atypical for a traumatic etiology and are suspicious for metastatic involvement given clinical context.
- Left 6th rib: A discrete focus of increased uptake in the posterior left 6th rib. Appearance more intense than would be expected for degenerative or post-traumatic change.
- Left 7th rib: Immediately inferior to the 6th rib lesion, a second discrete focus of increased uptake is identified in the posterior arc of the left 7th rib. The two lesions in adjacent left ribs are considered separate discrete foci of disease.

Femur:
- Right femoral neck: A focus of mildly increased radiotracer uptake is identified at the right femoral neck. The uptake is greater than the contralateral femoral neck, raising concern for early metastatic involvement. SPECT/CT correlation demonstrates no definitive sclerotic or lytic change at this site; this lesion may represent very early or permeative marrow disease not yet overtly apparent on CT.

Remaining Skeleton:
No additional suspicious foci of abnormal radiotracer uptake are identified in the calvarium, facial bones, mandible, cervical spine, remaining thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, sacrum, coccyx, sternum, clavicles, scapulae, humeri, remaining ribs, or lower extremity long bones. The hands and feet are not imaged in detail on this study.

Summary (Bone Scan): Eight discrete foci of abnormal radiotracer uptake as described above, distributed in the axial skeleton (T9, L2), pelvis (left iliac wing, right acetabulum), ribs (right 4th, left 6th, left 7th), and proximal right femur. In the context of a markedly elevated PSA of 187 ng/mL and histologically confirmed high-grade prostate adenocarcinoma, these findings are consistent with widespread osseous metastatic disease. No superscan. No findings to suggest cord compromise on SPECT/CT.

---

CT CHEST FINDINGS:

Lungs and Airways:
The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or infiltrates are identified. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. The airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally.

Mediastinum:
The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The mediastinum is midline. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No anterior mediastinal mass. The aorta is mildly atheromatous but not aneurysmal. Mild calcification of the coronary arteries, consistent with known cardiovascular risk factors.

Chest Wall / Osseous Structures (Chest):
No osteolytic or osteoblastic lesion is definitively identified in the visualized ribs or thoracic spine on CT alone; however, the bone scan findings in the right 4th rib, left 6th and 7th ribs, and T9 vertebral body are noted (see above). The CT component does not demonstrate macroscopic cortical destruction at these rib sites, suggesting early or permeative involvement.

Summary (Chest CT): No visceral pulmonary or mediastinal metastases. No pleural disease.

---

CT ABDOMEN FINDINGS:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No hepatic lesion is identified. No intrahepatic biliary dilation.

Gallbladder / Biliary:
Gallbladder is present and unremarkable. Common bile duct not dilated.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is within normal limits. No pancreatic mass or ductal dilation. Mild fatty infiltration of the pancreatic body/tail.

Spleen:
Spleen is normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands:
Both adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No adrenal mass or nodule. No adrenal thickening.

Kidneys:
The kidneys are symmetric in size. There is mild cortical thinning bilaterally, consistent with the known mild chronic kidney disease. No hydronephrosis. No renal mass. No renal calculi.

Retroperitoneum / Aorta:
The abdominal aorta is mildly atheromatous. No abdominal aortic aneurysm. Retroperitoneal lymph nodes: several common iliac lymph nodes are noted measuring up to 1.1 cm in short axis. These are at or just at the threshold for significance; while not definitively enlarged, their presence in the context of confirmed pelvic nodal disease warrants attention. No bulky retroperitoneal adenopathy.

Bowel:
Small bowel and large bowel are unremarkable. No bowel obstruction. No mesenteric adenopathy.

Peritoneum / Ascites:
No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or implants.

Summary (Abdomen CT): No hepatic, adrenal, or visceral abdominal metastases. Mildly enlarged common iliac lymph nodes (up to 1.1 cm) below the threshold for definitive pathological involvement but noted in context.

---

CT PELVIS FINDINGS:

Prostate Gland:
The prostate gland is enlarged with an estimated volume of approximately 48 cc. The gland demonstrates heterogeneous enhancement with irregular margins at the posterolateral aspects bilaterally, consistent with extraprostatic tumor extension. The fat planes between the posterior prostate and the seminal vesicles are effaced bilaterally, and both seminal vesicles demonstrate abnormal soft tissue thickening and enhancement, compatible with bilateral seminal vesicle invasion (cT3b). The neurovascular bundles bilaterally are not clearly separable from the tumor margin.

Bladder:
The urinary bladder is well distended. No bladder wall thickening or intraluminal mass. No hydronephrosis on the pelvic components.

Rectum / Sigmoid:
No rectal or sigmoid abnormality. No evidence of direct tumor extension into the rectum.

Pelvic Lymphadenopathy:
Multiple enlarged pelvic lymph nodes are identified:
- Right obturator lymph node chain: Dominant right obturator node measures 1.8 cm in short-axis diameter. Several additional right obturator chain nodes in the 0.8-1.1 cm range.
- Left external iliac lymph node: Enlarged node measuring 1.4 cm in short axis.
- Right external iliac lymph node: A node measuring 1.2 cm in short axis.
- Left obturator chain: Several borderline nodes measuring 0.8-0.9 cm.
No inguinal or femoral lymphadenopathy.

Osseous Structures (Pelvis):
As noted on bone scan, there is subtle sclerotic change in the left iliac wing and right acetabulum corresponding to sites of increased radiotracer uptake. No pathologic fracture. No collapse of the right acetabulum. The iliac cortices are intact.

Summary (Pelvis CT): Markedly enlarged prostate with extraprostatic extension and bilateral seminal vesicle invasion. Bilateral pelvic lymphadenopathy as described, most prominent in the right obturator chain (1.8 cm). No visceral pelvic involvement.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. MULTIFOCAL OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE: Eight discrete foci of abnormal radiotracer uptake on bone scan, involving the T9 and L2 vertebral bodies, left iliac wing, right acetabulum, right 4th rib, left 6th and 7th ribs, and right femoral neck. Findings are consistent with widespread osteoblastic metastases in the setting of a markedly elevated PSA and high-grade prostate adenocarcinoma. No superscan. No findings to suggest imminent spinal cord compromise on available imaging; clinical monitoring recommended.

2. LOCALLY ADVANCED PROSTATE CANCER WITH BILATERAL SEMINAL VESICLE INVASION: The prostate gland is enlarged (approximately 48 cc) with extraprostatic extension and bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, consistent with clinical staging of cT3b.

3. BILATERAL PELVIC LYMPHADENOPATHY: Multiple enlarged pelvic lymph nodes consistent with regional nodal metastatic involvement (N1), with the dominant right obturator node measuring 1.8 cm.

4. BORDERLINE COMMON ILIAC LYMPH NODES: Nodes up to 1.1 cm are noted at the common iliac level and are indeterminate; these may reflect early low-volume retroperitoneal involvement.

5. NO VISCERAL METASTASES: No hepatic, pulmonary, or adrenal metastases identified at this time.

Overall staging impression: Metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma with widespread osseous and pelvic nodal metastatic disease, consistent with AJCC stage IVB (M1b). No visceral metastases at this evaluation.

Attending Radiologist: [Signature on file]
Dictated and reviewed by: Attending staff radiologist, Nuclear Medicine and Body Imaging
Report status: Final
